title: What can wait until tomorrow?
subtitle: A five-prompt set for the night the unfinished list won't let you sleep.
excerpt: A five-prompt journal set for the night where the unfinished work is winning. Two warm-up prompts to name what's pulling, three narrower prompts to sort what can wait from what can't. About thirty minutes against a timer.
sourceNotes: Free-writing methodology: public-domain practice, popularised in modern form by Julia Cameron's morning pages (The Artist's Way, 1992). Theme adapted from Day 7 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Unhook from busyness". The five-prompt sort-the-list shape is original to the homemade.education library.

practice.whenToUse: Use in the half-hour before bed when the unfinished list is winning — emails, replies, school admin, the work-thing you didn't get to.

content.text: A five-prompt journal set for the night the unfinished work is winning. Five questions, two warm-up and three narrower. The point is to sort what can wait from what can't, before the mind tries to do that sort all night.
New to this style of journal practice? Read
journal prompts as practice
first.
The prompts
Warm-up
1. What's pulling at me right now?
The unfinished things at the top of the head. Half-replied emails, the school form, the thing the partner asked you to think about. List them, plain. Five minutes.
2. Where in the day did each one arrive?
Read the list back. Note when each item showed up. Often the items that arrived after 6pm are the ones the body is most willing to let go of overnight. Five minutes.
Closer in
3. Which of these has to be done before midnight?

Look squarely at what's actually time-bound. A reply that the other person is asleep for can wait. A school form that's due Friday can wait. Cross off the ones that don't have to happen tonight. Five minutes.
4. What is the worst that happens if I leave this one item until morning?
Pick the item that feels hardest to let go of. Name the actual worst case if it sits for nine hours. Often the worst case is small once it's named. Five minutes.
5. What are tomorrow's three priorities?
Pick three. Just three. Write them as a short list at the bottom of the page. The remaining items can stay on the list for the day after. Five minutes; less is fine.
After the prompts
Close the notebook on the page where the three priorities are written. Place the notebook on the bedside table. Tomorrow the list is there waiting; tonight the head can stop carrying it.
Where this practice comes from

Free-writing methodology comes from a long tradition — most modern readers will know it through Julia Cameron's morning pages (The Artist's Way, 1992). The theme of unhooking from busyness specifically at bedtime is adapted from Day 7 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025). The five-prompt sort-the-list shape is original to the homemade.education library.
